documents.text: Hypertension is defined as systolic BP ≥140 mmHg or diastolic BP ≥90 mmHg on at least two readings. Accurate diagnosis requires proper technique and out-of-office confirmation when appropriate.
First-line pharmacologic options for hypertension include ACE inhibitors, ARBs, thiazide diuretics, and calcium channel blockers. Choice depends on comorbidities, race, and tolerability.
For most adults with hypertension or elevated cardiovascular risk, treat to a target of <130/80 mmHg. For older adults with limited life expectancy, individualize targets.
Resistant hypertension is defined as BP above goal despite three antihypertensive agents including a diuretic. Exclude white-coat effect and medication nonadherence before escalating.
Sodium restriction to less than 2.3 g per day is recommended for all hypertensive patients. Further reduction to 1.5 g daily may provide additional benefit when achievable.
The DASH diet emphasizes fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and low-fat dairy while limiting saturated fat and cholesterol. It can lower systolic BP by 5–11 mmHg when combined with sodium reduction.
Aerobic exercise of at least 150 minutes per week at moderate intensity is recommended for blood pressure control. Dynamic resistance training may provide additional benefit.
Weight loss of 5–10% of body weight can significantly reduce blood pressure in overweight and obese individuals. Combined lifestyle intervention is more effective than diet or exercise alone.
Lisinopril is an ACE inhibitor used for hypertension and heart failure. Common adverse effects include cough, hyperkalemia, and angioedema. Monitor renal function and potassium.
Ramipril is indicated for hypertension, heart failure, and post-MI. Reduce dose when CrCl is below 50 mL/min. Contraindicated in pregnancy due to fetal toxicity.
Enalapril has a rapid onset and is used for hypertension and heart failure. Avoid in bilateral renal artery stenosis. May cause cough and hyperkalemia; monitor in renal impairment.
ACE inhibitor-induced cough is a class effect mediated by bradykinin accumulation. Switching to an ARB typically resolves cough while preserving RAS blockade.
ACE inhibitors can cause acute kidney injury in bilateral renal artery stenosis or volume depletion. Check baseline creatinine and potassium before and after initiation.
ACE inhibitors are preferred in diabetic patients with albuminuria or cardiovascular disease. They slow progression of diabetic kidney disease and reduce cardiovascular events.
Losartan is an ARB used for hypertension and diabetic nephropathy. It has a uricosuric effect. Avoid in pregnancy; monitor potassium and renal function.
Valsartan is indicated for hypertension, heart failure, and post-MI. Dose adjustment needed in hepatic impairment. Do not use with aliskiren in diabetes or renal impairment.
Telmisartan has a long half-life allowing once-daily dosing. Used for hypertension; may have PPAR-gamma activity. Avoid in pregnancy and bilateral renal artery stenosis.
ARBs and ACE inhibitors have similar cardiovascular and renal benefits. ARBs are preferred when ACE inhibitor cough or angioedema occurs. Do not combine ACE and ARB routinely.
ARBs are first-line for diabetic kidney disease with albuminuria. They reduce proteinuria and slow GFR decline. Use maximum tolerated dose for renoprotection.
Type 2 diabetes is diagnosed by HbA1c ≥6.5%, fasting glucose ≥126 mg/dL, or 2-hour glucose ≥200 mg/dL on OGTT. Confirm with repeat testing unless classic symptoms present.
Metformin is first-line pharmacotherapy for type 2 diabetes unless contraindicated. It does not cause hypoglycemia as monotherapy. Hold before contrast and in acute illness.
For most nonpregnant adults with diabetes, target HbA1c is below 7%. Less stringent targets may be appropriate for limited life expectancy, advanced complications, or hypoglycemia risk.
In type 2 diabetes with established cardiovascular disease, SGLT2 inhibitors or GLP-1 receptor agonists with proven cardiovascular benefit are recommended regardless of HbA1c.
In diabetic patients with chronic kidney disease, SGLT2 inhibitors are recommended when eGFR is adequate. They reduce kidney failure and cardiovascular events.
In diabetes with hypertension, target blood pressure is <130/80 mmHg. ACE inhibitors or ARBs are preferred for those with albuminuria or cardiovascular disease.
Pooled cohort equations estimate 10-year risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Use for adults 40–79 without known CVD to guide statin and aspirin decisions.
High-intensity statin therapy is recommended for clinical ASCVD and for primary prevention when 10-year risk is ≥20%. Moderate-intensity is an option when high-intensity is not tolerated.
Hypertension is a major modifiable risk factor for stroke, MI, and heart failure. Even modest BP reduction significantly lowers cardiovascular events.
Diabetes substantially increases cardiovascular risk. Comprehensive risk factor management—glycemia, blood pressure, lipids, and antiplatelet therapy—is essential.
Lifestyle modification reduces cardiovascular risk: smoking cessation, healthy diet, physical activity, and weight management. These should accompany pharmacotherapy.
White-coat hypertension is elevated BP in clinic with normal out-of-office readings. Confirm with home or ambulatory monitoring before starting lifelong therapy.
Masked hypertension is normal clinic BP with elevated home or ambulatory readings. It carries cardiovascular risk similar to sustained hypertension and is underdiagnosed.
Calcium channel blockers are first-line for hypertension, especially in older adults and Black patients. Dihydropyridines are preferred for BP; non-DHPs for rate control.
Thiazide and thiazide-like diuretics are first-line for hypertension. Chlorthalidone may be preferred over hydrochlorothiazide for superior 24-hour BP control and outcome data.
Benazepril is an ACE inhibitor used for hypertension. It is a prodrug converted to benazeprilat. Reduce dose in renal impairment; contraindicated in pregnancy.
Quinapril is an ACE inhibitor for hypertension and heart failure. Dose adjustment required when CrCl is below 60 mL/min. Monitor for angioedema, especially in first month.
Irbesartan is an ARB for hypertension and diabetic nephropathy with macroalbuminuria. It has a long half-life. Avoid in pregnancy; monitor potassium in renal impairment.
Candesartan is used for hypertension and heart failure. It is an active drug not requiring metabolism. Do not use with aliskiren in diabetes; avoid in pregnancy.
SGLT2 inhibitors lower glucose by reducing reabsorption in the proximal tubule. They reduce heart failure hospitalizations and kidney progression in type 2 diabetes.
GLP-1 receptor agonists improve glycemia and promote weight loss. Those with proven cardiovascular benefit should be considered in type 2 diabetes with established CVD.
Limit alcohol to no more than one drink per day for women and two for men to support blood pressure control. Excessive intake raises BP and interferes with medications.
Smoking cessation is critical for cardiovascular risk reduction. Each risk factor guideline recommends cessation; consider behavioral support and pharmacotherapy.
Hypertensive urgency is severely elevated BP without acute organ damage. Lower BP gradually over hours to days; avoid rapid reduction. Reassess medications and adherence.
Hypertensive emergency is severe BP elevation with acute organ damage. Treat in ICU with intravenous agents; reduce mean arterial pressure by no more than 25% in the first hour.
ACE inhibitor angioedema can occur at any time during therapy. Discontinue permanently and avoid ARBs in the same patient due to cross-reactivity risk.
Hypoglycemia is a limiting factor in diabetes management. Individualize targets and drug choice to minimize hypoglycemia, especially in elderly and those with long duration.
Aspirin for primary prevention of ASCVD should be individualized in adults 40–70 at increased risk without bleeding risk. Do not use routinely in adults over 70.
After starting or changing antihypertensive therapy, reassess within one month. Once at goal, follow up every 3–6 months. More frequent monitoring if recent change or comorbidities.
ARBs can cause hyperkalemia, especially with renal impairment or when combined with potassium-sparing drugs. Monitor potassium; consider dose reduction or discontinuation if persistent.
Screen for prediabetes and type 2 diabetes in adults 35–70 who are overweight or obese. Use FPG, HbA1c, or OGTT. Repeat every 3 years if normal.
